[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker who observes a pending (Authorization, Signature) pair in the mempool front-run the victim's relayer by submitting the identical calldata first, consuming nonce[authorization.authorizer] before the victim's tx lands, causing the victim's tx to revert with InvalidNonce() and permanently blocking the operator authorization needed for take/repay/withdraw? Preconditions: victim has signed an Authorization with nonce==0 and broadcast it via a relayer; attacker monitors mempool. Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization{authorizer=V, authorized=OP, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=T+1d}; (2) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) first; (3) victim's relayer tx reverts InvalidNonce(); (4) OP cannot call Midnight.take/repay/withdraw on behalf of V. Invariant tested: a valid signature submitted by the authorizer or their relayer must not be consumable by an adversary before the intended submitter. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS against take, repay, withdraw, withdrawCollateral for any user relying on gasless authorization. Proof idea: Foundry test with vm.prank(attacker) submitting the same (auth, sig) before the relayer; assert relayer tx reverts InvalidNonce and Midnight.isAuthorized(V, OP) == false.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an already-authorized delegate Bob (isAuthorized[Alice][Bob]==true in Midnight) continuously consume Alice's nonce in EcrecoverAuthorizer by signing and submitting no-op Authorization structs (e.g., re-authorizing Bob himself) on Alice's behalf, permanently preventing Alice from ever using EcrecoverAuthorizer for gasless authorization? Preconditions: Alice has called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(Bob, true, Alice); Bob is malicious. Call sequence: (1) Bob signs Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=Bob, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=T+1d}; (2) Bob calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig); (3) nonce[Alice] increments; (4) repeat indefinitely. Invariant tested: an authorized delegate must not be able to grief the authorizer's EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce without the authorizer's consent. Scoped impact: permanent DoS of gasless authorization for Alice, blocking any future operator setup via EcrecoverAuthorizer. Proof idea: fuzz loop where Bob submits N no-op authorizations; assert nonce[Alice] == N and Alice cannot submit her own authorization without knowing the current nonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate Bob use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke a legitimate operator Carol's authorization (isAuthorized[Alice][Carol]) by signing Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=Carol, isAuthorized=false} and submitting it, causing Carol's pending take/repay/withdraw calls on behalf of Alice to revert with Unauthorized()? Invariant tested: authorization revocation via EcrecoverAuthorizer must require the authorizer's own signature, not a delegate's unilateral action. Scoped impact: DoS of repay/withdraw/take for Alice's legitimate operators. Proof idea: unit test asserting Carol's repay reverts after Bob submits the revocation authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate Bob use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant a new unauthorized address Eve full control over Alice's Midnight state (isAuthorized[Alice][Eve]=true) by signing Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=Eve, isAuthorized=true}, enabling Eve to call Midnight.withdrawCollateral/withdraw/setConsumed on behalf of Alice? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate Bob use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the ratifier's authorization (isAuthorized[Alice][ratifier]=false) by signing Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=ratifier, isAuthorized=false}, causing all subsequent Midnight.take() calls for Alice's offers to revert with RatifierUnauthorized()? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit ECDSA signature malleability (flipping s to secp256k1.n - s and adjusting v) to produce a second valid signature for the same Authorization struct, front-running the victim's submission and consuming the nonce before the victim's original signature lands? Preconditions: victim has signed an Authorization and broadcast it; attacker computes the malleable form. Call sequence: (1) victim signs (v, r, s); (2) attacker computes (v', r, secp256k1.n - s) which also passes ecrecover; (3) attacker submits malleable sig first; (4) victim's tx reverts InvalidNonce(). Invariant tested: each Authorization nonce must be consumable at most once; malleable signatures must not enable double-submission. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS of gasless authorization setup. Proof idea: compute malleable signature in Foundry and assert both recover to the same signer address; assert second submission reverts InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with authorization.authorizer == address(0) and a crafted (v, r, s) such that ecrecover returns address(0), bypassing the signer != address(0) check if the check ordering allows it, and calling Midnight.setIsAuthorized on behalf of address(0)? Call sequence: (1) attacker constructs Authorization{authorizer=address(0), authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}; (2) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig); (3) check: does signer==address(0) pass the require(signer != address(0)) guard? Scoped impact: potential authorization of attacker as operator for address(0) (low direct impact but tests guard correctness). [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who has obtained a valid (Authorization, Signature) pair with a far-future deadline hold it and submit it at a strategically chosen block to DoS the victim's operator at a critical moment (e.g., when a position is near liquidation and the operator needs to repay), by submitting a revocation authorization (isAuthorized=false) just before the operator's repay tx? Preconditions: victim previously signed an Authorization{isAuthorized=false, deadline=T+365d} and it leaked; attacker holds it. Call sequence: (1) victim's position approaches liquidation threshold; (2) attacker submits the revocation authorization; (3) Midnight.isAuthorized(victim, operator) == false; (4) operator's repay tx reverts Unauthorized(); (5) position becomes liquidatable. [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker batch-grief a victim's EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce by using Midnight.multicall to submit multiple setIsAuthorized calls in a single transaction, each consuming one nonce increment, making it economically cheap to advance the nonce by hundreds in one tx? Preconditions: attacker has obtained multiple valid (Authorization, Signature) pairs for consecutive nonces (e.g., from a compromised relayer cache or by acting as an authorized delegate). Call sequence: (1) attacker constructs multicall with N calls to ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for nonces 0..N-1; (2) submits in one tx; (3) nonce[victim] == N; (4) victim's pending authorization with nonce 0 reverts InvalidNonce(). Invariant tested: nonce griefing cost must be proportional to the number of valid signatures obtained, not reducible via batching. Scoped impact: low-cost bulk DoS of gasless authorization. Proof idea: Foundry test with multicall batching N delegate-signed authorizations; assert nonce advances N in one tx.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the liquidator's authorization (isAuthorized[borrower][liquidatorOperator]=false) just before a liquidation is attempted, causing the liquidation to fail and leaving an unhealthy position unliquidated? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with block.timestamp == authorization.deadline (exact boundary), and does the check require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) allow or reject this, potentially causing off-by-one DoS if integrations assume strict inequality? Preconditions: victim signs Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp of the submission block. Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization{deadline=T}; (2) at block.timestamp==T, attacker submits; (3) check: block.timestamp <= deadline is true; (4) nonce consumed; (5) victim's own submission at same block also reverts InvalidNonce(). Proof idea: unit test with vm.warp to exact deadline; assert first submission succeeds and second reverts InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer's nonce mapping is separate from Midnight's isAuthorized mapping to create a state where the EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce is far ahead of what the victim expects, causing all victim-signed authorizations to fail with InvalidNonce() while Midnight's isAuthorized state is unchanged? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use a flashloan callback to atomically (1) obtain a loan, (2) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to consume a victim's nonce, and (3) repay the loan, all in one transaction, making the nonce-griefing attack essentially free (only gas cost)? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate of Alice use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves authorization for a second market's operations by signing Authorization{authorizer=Alice, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true} and then calling Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, Alice) to cancel all of Alice's pending offers, causing DoS of all take operations against Alice's offers? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with authorization.authorized == address(0) to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(0), true, authorizer), and does this create any exploitable state (e.g., if any code path checks isAuthorized[user][address(0)])? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorizer == authorization.authorized (self-authorization), consuming the nonce without any meaningful state change in Midnight, and use this as a cheap nonce-burning mechanism to grief the authorizer's future gasless authorization attempts? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker create a circular authorization chain (A authorizes B, B authorizes C via EcrecoverAuthorizer, C authorizes A via EcrecoverAuthorizer) and use this to perform unexpected state mutations on A's Midnight position without A's direct consent? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a victim's authorization setup that is required before a time-sensitive take (e.g., an offer expiring in the next block), consuming the nonce so the operator cannot be authorized in time, causing the offer to expire before the take can be executed? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke a repay operator's authorization just as a borrower's position crosses the liquidation threshold, preventing the operator from calling Midnight.repay and forcing the position into liquidation? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with a nonce value of type(uint256).max - 1, causing nonce[authorizer] to overflow to 0 on the next increment, potentially allowing replay of old nonce-0 signatures? Call sequence: (1) nonce[authorizer] is advanced to type(uint256).max - 1; (2) attacker submits Authorization{nonce=type(uint256).max-1}; (3) nonce[authorizer] becomes type(uint256).max; (4) next increment: does nonce overflow to 0? Invariant tested: nonce must never overflow and must never allow replay of previously used nonces. Scoped impact: signature replay enabling unauthorized authorization changes. [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that the EIP-712 domain separator uses block.chainid dynamically (not cached at construction) to submit a signature on a chain where block.chainid has changed (e.g., after a hard fork), causing unexpected behavior if the chain ID changes between signing and submission? Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization on chain with chainid=1; (2) hard fork changes chainid to 2; (3) domain separator changes; (4) victim's signature is invalid on new chain; (5) victim cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer until re-signing. Proof idea: Foundry test with vm.chainId change; assert old signature reverts InvalidSignature on new chain.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a repay callback contract (isAuthorized[borrower][repayCallback]=false), causing Midnight.repay calls that use that callback to revert Unauthorized(), DoS-ing the borrower's ability to repay via callback-sourced funds? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit multiple Authorization structs with the same nonce but different (authorized, isAuthorized) values in the same block, and does the nonce increment in require(authorization.nonce == nonce[authorization.authorizer]++) correctly prevent double-spending within the same block? Call sequence: (1) attacker submits Authorization{nonce=N, authorized=A, isAuthorized=true}; (2) attacker submits Authorization{nonce=N, authorized=B, isAuthorized=true} in same block; (3) second tx reverts InvalidNonce(). Invariant tested: each nonce must be consumable exactly once even within the same block. Scoped impact: double-authorization or nonce replay.
